0a. Study `specs/*` with up to 500 parallel subagents to learn the application specifications.
0b. If @TASK.md exists, read it — this is your sole task for this iteration. Skip @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md for task selection. Otherwise, study @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md.
0c. Study @AGENTS.md for build commands and code patterns.

1. If @TASK.md exists, that is your SOLE task — implement it, do not consult @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md for task selection. Otherwise, follow @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md and choose the most important item to address. Before making changes, search the codebase (don't assume not implemented) using Sonnet subagents. You may use up to 500 parallel Sonnet subagents for searches/reads and only 1 Sonnet subagent for build/tests. Use Opus subagents when complex reasoning is needed (debugging, architectural decisions).
2. After implementing functionality or resolving problems, run the tests for that unit of code that was improved. If functionality is missing then it's your job to add it as per the application specifications. Ultrathink.
3. When you discover issues, immediately update @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md with your findings using a subagent. When resolved, update and remove the item.
4. When the tests pass: if working from @TASK.md, delete @TASK.md (its removal signals completion to the loop); otherwise, update @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md. Then `git add -A` then `git commit` with a message describing the changes. After the commit, `git push`.

99999. Important: When authoring documentation, capture the why — tests and implementation importance.
999999. Important: Single sources of truth, no migrations/adapters. If tests unrelated to your work fail, resolve them as part of the increment.
9999999. As soon as there are no build or test errors create a git tag. If there are no git tags start at 0.0.0 and increment patch by 1 for example 0.0.1 if 0.0.0 does not exist.
99999999. You may add extra logging if required to debug issues.
999999999. Keep @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md current with learnings using a subagent — future work depends on this to avoid duplicating efforts. Update especially after finishing your turn.
9999999999. When you learn something new about how to build/run the project, update @AGENTS.md using a subagent but keep it brief. For example if you run commands multiple times before learning the correct command then that file should be updated.
99999999999. For any bugs you notice, resolve them or document them in @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md using a subagent even if it is unrelated to the current piece of work.
999999999999. Implement functionality completely. Placeholders and stubs waste efforts and time redoing the same work.
9999999999999. When @IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md becomes large periodically clean out the items that are completed from the file using a subagent.
99999999999999. If you find inconsistencies in the specs/* then use an Opus subagent with 'ultrathink' requested to update the specs.
999999999999999. IMPORTANT: Keep @AGENTS.md operational only — status updates and progress notes belong in IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md. A bloated AGENTS.md pollutes every future loop's context.

## src/tools/mod.rs

### Macro

`tools!` — declarative macro that generates `all_tool_schemas() -> Vec<serde_json::Value>` from a list of `(name, description, schema)` triples.

### Tool registry (generated by macro)

Five tools registered: `Read`, `Glob`, `Bash`, `Edit`, `Grep`.

### Public enum

`ToolEffect` (Copy, PartialEq):
- `Pure`
- `Mutating`

### Public functions

`all_tool_schemas() -> Vec<serde_json::Value>` — returns 5 JSON schemas with PascalCase names.

`tool_effect(name: &str) -> ToolEffect`:
- `"Read" | "Glob" | "Grep"` → Pure
- `"Bash" | "Edit"` → Mutating
- unknown → Mutating (fail-safe)

`dispatch_tool(name: &str, input: &Value, stream_cb: &mut dyn FnMut(&str)) -> Result<String, String>` — dispatches to `read_exec`, `glob_exec`, `bash_exec`, `edit_exec`, `grep_exec`, or returns error.

### Private functions

`read_exec(input)` — reads file, max 1MB, NUL-byte binary detection in first 8KB, returns binary placeholder or UTF-8 string.

`glob_exec(input)` — calls `expand_braces`, then `glob::glob` (no shell subprocess). Cap at 1000 entries. Deduplication via `HashSet`.

`expand_braces(pattern: &str) -> Vec<String>` — expands first brace group only (e.g. `**/*.{rs,toml}`).

`bash_exec(input, stream_cb)` — checks `is_denied_command`, spawns `bash -c`, two reader threads (stdout/stderr merged into mpsc channel), 120s deadline loop, 1MB output cap (`BASH_OUTPUT_LIMIT`). Returns error on timeout, truncation, or non-zero exit.

`edit_exec(input)` — handles three modes: create (empty `old_str`, file missing), append (empty `old_str`, file exists), or single-exact/replace_all edit. Max 100KB (`EDIT_SIZE_LIMIT`).

`grep_exec(input)` — uses `rg` with `--max-count=50 --line-number --no-heading --color=never`. Availability cached via `OnceLock<bool>`. Exit code 1 → "No matches found".

`normalize_command(cmd) -> String` — lowercase + whitespace-normalize.
`is_denied_command(cmd: &str) -> bool` — checks against `BASH_DENY_LIST`.

### Constants

`BASH_DENY_LIST: &[&str]` — 10 patterns: `rm -rf /`, `rm -fr /`, `rm -rf /*`, `rm -fr /*`, fork bomb, `dd if=/dev`, `mkfs`, `chmod 777 /`, `git push --force`, `git push -f`.
`BASH_OUTPUT_LIMIT: usize = 1_048_576` (1MB)
`LIMIT: usize = 1000` (glob cap, local to `glob_exec`)
`EDIT_SIZE_LIMIT: u64 = 102_400` (100KB, local to `edit_exec`)

### Public methods on HookRunner

`filter_null_input_tool_use(blocks: Vec<ContentBlock>) -> Vec<ContentBlock>` — removes ToolUse blocks with null input; returns `["[Response truncated]"]` if all removed.

`classify_max_tokens(blocks, continuation_count) -> MaxTokensAction` — `[Response truncated]` → BreakEmpty; has ToolUse → DispatchTools; text-only under cap → Continue; text-only at cap → BreakCapReached.

`run_pre_dispatch(hooks, id, name, input, iterations, consecutive_block_count, total_block_count) -> PreDispatchResult` (async) — null-input check (no counter increment); pre-hook; increments both counters on block; resets consecutive on allow; returns ThresholdTripped when either limit exceeded.

`run_post_dispatch(hooks, name, input, content, is_error, iterations, verbose) -> bool` (async) — formats result display, runs post-hook, returns true if Signal received.

`log_tool_dispatch(name, input, verbose)`, `threshold_stop_reason(consecutive) -> TurnStopReason`, `use_color() -> bool`, `atty_check() -> bool`.

`dispatch_to_tool_result(id, name, input) -> ContentBlock` — sync wrapper for use in `spawn_blocking`.

`join_spawned_futures(futures, slots, tool_ids)` (async) — joins `Vec<JoinHandle>`, fills slots, replaces panics with error ToolResult.

`format_tool_result_display(result, is_error, verbose) -> String` — error: 200-char preview; verbose non-error: full text; non-verbose non-error: char count.

`truncate_json(value, max_len) -> String`

### Main function

`async fn main()` — parses CLI; builds `AnthropicClient` and `SessionWriter`; loads `HookRunner` from `.forgeflare/hooks.toml`; clears convergence state; loads project instructions appended to system prompt; detects piped vs interactive stdin via `atty_check()`; piped: reads all stdin and calls `run_turn` once; interactive: REPL loop until EOF or `exit`/`quit`; calls `session.write_context()` on exit.

### Core loop — `run_turn` (async)

Handles one user turn. Inner loop:
1. `trim_if_needed` on each iteration.
2. Retry loop (up to `MAX_RETRIES=4`) with exponential backoff `[2,4,8,16]s`, respecting `retry-after` header (capped at 60s). Permanent errors break immediately.
3. On `MaxTokens`: classify with `classify_max_tokens`; inject continuation prompt or break.
4. Tool dispatch: if all tools in batch are Pure → parallel path (pre-dispatch all, `spawn_blocking` for allowed, `join_all`); else → sequential path.
5. Both paths run `run_pre_dispatch` (null-input safety, hooks, block counters) and `run_post_dispatch` (hooks, convergence signal detection).
6. Threshold trip (consecutive or total block limit) → pop assistant message, break with `BlockLimit*` reason.
7. Convergence signal → break with `ConvergenceSignal`.
8. After loop: `hooks.run_stop(turn_stop_reason.as_str(), tool_iterations, total_tokens)`.

## specs/coding-agent.md — Unified Rust Coding Agent

Status: Complete. Target: single binary, streaming, <950 production lines.

Requirements:

R1. Core Loop — REPL: read input, call Claude API, dispatch tools, repeat. Streaming SSE from day 1. Conversation history managed as a context array.

R2. HTTP Client — Use `reqwest` directly (no Anthropic crate). POST to `/v1/messages`. Handle SSE. Expose `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.

R3. Tool Registry Pattern — `tools!` macro generates `all_tool_schemas()`. `dispatch_tool()` is hand-written to support per-tool signatures (bash gets a streaming callback, others do not).

R4. Five Tools — Read (file contents, binary/size limits), Glob (path+recursive, file listing), Bash (command+cwd, timeout, streaming via callback), Edit (path+old_str+new_str+replace_all; exact match default; empty old_str on missing file = create with mkdir; empty old_str on existing file = append), Grep (pattern+path+file_type+case_sensitive, shells out to `rg`).

R5. CLI Interface — Single binary. `--verbose`, `--model` (default: claude-opus-4-6), `--max-tokens` (default: 16384, max 128K). Stdin as single prompt if piped. Exit on EOF or "exit".

R6. Error Handling — `thiserror` for `AgentError` in api.rs. Tool errors as `Result<String, String>`. Display errors, continue loop, no panics.

R7. Streaming Architecture — Collect SSE into response buffer. Check `stop_reason` for `tool_use` vs `end_turn`. Dispatch tools or display response accordingly.

R8. Subagent Awareness (Deferred) — No SubagentContext types. `StopReason` enum retained for loop control only.

Success Criteria: binary compiles, tests pass, clippy clean, formatted, can chat/read/list/bash/edit/search, under 950 production lines, streaming visible in real-time.

Notable implementation notes: 720KB byte budget for conversation trim (~180K tokens). Bash deny-list blocks destructive commands (rm -rf /, fork bombs, etc.) with whitespace normalization. 50-iteration tool loop safety limit. 30s connect / 300s request timeouts.

## specs/api-retry.md — API Retry for Transient Failures

Status: Complete. Target: retry transient errors (429, 503, 529, network timeouts) with exponential backoff.

Supersedes coding-agent.md's "No automatic retry" principle.

Requirements:

R1. Classify API Errors as Transient or Permanent — By variant:
- `HttpError { status, .. }`: transient = 429, 503, 529, unknown >= 500; permanent = 400, 401, 403, unknown 4xx
- `StreamTransient(String)`: always transient. Covers SSE `error` events with `overloaded_error`, `api_error` types; stream ended without stop_reason
- `StreamParse(String)`: always permanent. Malformed JSON, protocol violations, `invalid_request_error` SSE events
- `Api(reqwest::Error)`: transient if `e.is_timeout()` or `e.is_connect()`; otherwise permanent
- `Json(serde_json::Error)`: always permanent

SSE error classification: inspect `p["error"]["type"]` (nested, NOT top-level `p["type"]`). `overloaded_error`, `api_error`, `rate_limit_error` → `StreamTransient`. `invalid_request_error` → `StreamParse`. Unknown types default to `StreamTransient`.

R2. Retry with Exponential Backoff — Backoff schedule: 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s. Max 4 retries (5 total calls). If `HttpError` has `retry_after: Some(n)`, use n seconds instead (capped at 60s). After max retries: fall through to existing error handling.

R3. Preserve Existing Error Path for Permanent Failures — Permanent errors: immediate `recover_conversation()` + break. No behavioral change for 400/401/403.

R4. Logging — `[retry] Attempt {n}/4: {status} — waiting {delay}s` (1-indexed). `[retry] Using retry-after: {n}s` when header overrides backoff.

Architecture: Add `HttpError { status: u16, retry_after: Option<u64>, body: String }` variant to `AgentError`. Add `StreamTransient(String)` variant. Add `classify_error(e: &AgentError) -> ErrorClass` in api.rs. Retry loop lives in main.rs, wraps entire `send_message()` call (not inside Err arm).

Success Criteria: 429 retried after retry-after seconds, 503/529 with exponential backoff, network timeout retried, 400 triggers immediate break, max 4 retries, attempts logged, `recover_conversation()` still called on final failure.

## specs/token-aware-trim.md — Token-Aware Context Trimming

Status: Complete. Target: use actual API token counts to gate context trim decisions.

Requirements:

R1. Track Last Known Input Tokens — After each successful `send_message()`, store `usage.input_tokens` as `last_input_tokens: u64`. Initialize to 0 at start of each outer loop iteration.

R2. Token-Based Trim Decision — Before calling `trim_conversation()`:
- `last_input_tokens == 0`: fall through to byte-based trim (no token data yet, or usage absent)
- `last_input_tokens > 0 && last_input_tokens < TRIM_THRESHOLD` (120K): skip trim entirely
- `last_input_tokens >= TRIM_THRESHOLD`: run `trim_conversation()`

R3. Context Window Constant — `MODEL_CONTEXT_TOKENS: u64 = 200_000`. `TRIM_THRESHOLD: u64 = MODEL_CONTEXT_TOKENS * 60 / 100` (120K tokens). 60% threshold leaves 80K headroom for tool results. Do NOT raise to 80% — that leaves only 40K headroom, which large file reads can exceed.

R4. Preserve Byte-Based Safety Net — `trim_conversation()` itself unchanged. Token check is a gate before the byte trim, not a replacement.

Success Criteria: first API call always runs byte trim, subsequent calls skip trim when under 120K tokens, run trim when at or above 120K, `last_input_tokens` resets to 0 on new user input, existing trim tests pass.

## specs/maxtoken-continuation.md — MaxTokens Continuation

Status: Complete. Target: continue from MaxTokens truncation instead of breaking the inner loop.

Requirements:

R1. Detect Continuable Truncation — After MaxTokens handling (filter null-input tool_use blocks, inject placeholder if empty): check if remaining content is text-only (no pending tool_use blocks with valid inputs). Text-only = continuable. Tool_use blocks present = fall through to dispatch.

R2. Inject Continuation Prompt — Push User message "Continue from where you left off." AND log via `session.append_user_turn()`. Do NOT increment `tool_iterations`. If response is empty after filtering (only placeholder), do NOT attempt continuation — break immediately. Loop back to API call.

R3. Cap Continuations — Maximum 3 continuation attempts. Track via `continuation_count`. After 3, fall through to existing break.

R4. Logging — `[continue] Response truncated at max_tokens, requesting continuation ({n}/3)`. `[continue] Max continuations reached, breaking`.

Architecture (canonical control flow, exact restructure specified):

```text
if stop_reason == EndTurn { break; }
if stop_reason == MaxTokens {
    // filter null-input + inject placeholder (existing code)
    if is_empty { break; }
    let has_valid_tools = ...;
    if has_valid_tools { /* fall through to dispatch */ }
    else if continuation_count < 3 { continuation_count += 1; /* push user msg, log, continue */ }
    else { /* log cap, break */ }
}
// Tool dispatch runs for ToolUse and MaxTokens-with-valid-tools
```

Success Criteria: text-only MaxTokens triggers continuation, tool_use MaxTokens falls to dispatch, continuation itself hitting MaxTokens increments count toward cap, empty MaxTokens breaks immediately, `continuation_count` resets on new user input (outer loop), continuation logged to JSONL via `session.append_user_turn()`, cap at 3, `continuation_count` does NOT increment `tool_iterations`, does NOT reset on tool_result User messages.

## specs/tool-parallelism.md — Parallel Dispatch for Pure Tools

Status: Complete. Target: classify tools by side effects, execute read-only tools concurrently.

Requirements:

R1. Tool Effect Classification — `pub ToolEffect` enum: `Pure` and `Mutating`. Map: `Read → Pure`, `Glob → Pure`, `Grep → Pure`, `Bash → Mutating`, `Edit → Mutating`. `pub fn tool_effect(name: &str) -> ToolEffect` in `tools/mod.rs`. Unknown tool names default to `Mutating`.

R2. Batch Classification — Before dispatching: if ALL tools in batch are `Pure`, dispatch concurrently. If ANY tool is `Mutating`, dispatch entire batch sequentially.

R3. Concurrent Dispatch — Use `futures_util::future::join_all()` (from `futures_util`, NOT `futures` umbrella crate). Parallel path MUST replicate null-input guard. Log each tool name BEFORE spawning. Collect results in original order. Failed individual tool does not cancel siblings.

R4. Preserve Sequential Fallback — `Mutating` path is exactly the existing `for block in ...` loop. Bash streaming callback continues as-is.

Architecture: `dispatch_tool()` is sync; wrap in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || dispatch_tool(...))`. Streaming callback is `&mut dyn FnMut(&str)` (not Send), so pure path uses no-op `&mut |_: &str| {}` inside closures. `join_all` returns `Vec<Result<ContentBlock, JoinError>>`. `tool_iterations += 1` must be present after BOTH parallel and sequential paths. The `tool_results.is_empty()` break guard preserved after both paths.

Success Criteria: 3 concurrent Reads complete faster than sequential, mixed batch dispatches sequentially, results maintain original ordering, individual tool errors don't cancel siblings, unknown tool names classified as Mutating, `JoinError` produces valid ToolResult error, per-tool logging present in parallel path, batch of 1 pure tool works.

## specs/hooks.md — Hook Dispatch Layer

Status: Complete. Target: shell-based hook system with PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop events.

Requirements:

R1. Three Hook Events — `PreToolUse` (before execution; guard can allow/block; observe always runs), `PostToolUse` (after execution; can signal convergence), `Stop` (on loop end; 7 reason values: `end_turn`, `iteration_limit`, `api_error`, `continuation_cap`, `block_limit_consecutive`, `block_limit_total`, `convergence_signal`).

R2. Hook Configuration — TOML at `.forgeflare/hooks.toml`. Fields: `event`, `command`, `match_tool` (optional, exact match only), `phase` (`guard`/`observe`, PreToolUse only), `timeout_ms` (default 5000, Stop hooks default 3000). Missing file → no-op zero overhead.

R3. Hook Contract — Executables. JSON on stdin, JSON on stdout. Stderr forwarded. Exit 0 = valid JSON. Non-zero = failure. `tool_iterations` is per-batch counter (same counter as 50-iteration ceiling, increments once per API response cycle). PostToolUse `result` field capped at 5120 bytes with truncation marker (first 2560 + `"\n... (truncated...)...\n"` + last 2560, byte boundaries via `floor_char_boundary`).

R4. PreToolUse Phase Model — Guard hooks: can return `allow` or `block`; run in declaration order; short-circuit on block; fail-closed (timeout/non-zero exit/invalid JSON → Block). Observe hooks: always run even after guard block; receive `blocked`/`blocked_by`/`block_reason` fields; return value ignored; fail-open. Distinct error messages for: intentional block vs. timeout vs. crash vs. invalid JSON.

R5. PostToolUse and Stop are Fail-Open — Failures logged, proceed.

R6. Block Counters with Inner Loop Break — `consecutive_block_count`: increments on every guard block, resets to 0 on every successful dispatch, threshold `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_BLOCKS = 3`. `total_block_count`: increments on every guard block, never resets within inner loop, threshold `MAX_TOTAL_BLOCKS = 10`. When threshold hit: `conversation.pop()` (NOT `recover_conversation()`), break. Aborted batch does NOT increment `tool_iterations`. Block threshold takes unconditional precedence over `signal_break`.

R7. PostToolUse Signal as Control Flow — `PostToolResult::Signal` → set `signal_break` flag. After batch completes, send tool_results, then break WITHOUT `recover_conversation()`. Stop hook fires with `convergence_signal`. Multiple PostToolUse hooks run in order; first Signal controls return value but subsequent hooks still execute and write observations.

R8. Convergence State Protocol — `.forgeflare/convergence.json` with `observations` array (PostToolUse signals) and `final` key (Stop). Deleted at startup (clean slate). Atomic writes via same-directory temp file + `fs::rename` (NOT `/tmp/`). Single read-modify-write per `run_post_tool_use` call after all hooks complete. Write failures logged as warnings, do not affect return value.

R9. Hooks Wrap Dispatch, Not Inside It — `dispatch_tool` remains sync. Hook calls in `main.rs`. Sequential path: guard → observe → dispatch or block → post-hook. Parallel path: pre-allocate `Vec<Option<ContentBlock>>` slots + `blocked_flags: Vec<bool>`; guard/observe per tool sequentially before spawning; `join_all` dispatched futures; post-hook loop skipping blocked slots. `consecutive_block_count` resets at guard-allow time (before spawn), not execution-completion time.

Architecture changes: new `src/hooks.rs` (~250 LOC), `main.rs` delta (~60 LOC), add `toml` to Cargo.toml. `dispatch_tool` signature unchanged.

Success Criteria: 52 explicit criteria covering guard blocking, fail-closed behavior, observe hook behavior, PostToolUse signals, Stop reasons, block counters, convergence state atomicity, parallel path slot ordering, `match_tool` exact matching, and more.

## specs/glob-shell-injection.md — Replace Shell Injection in Glob Tool

Status: Active. Target: eliminate shell injection in `glob_exec` by replacing bash shell-out with the `glob` Rust crate.

Problem: `glob_exec` interpolates user-supplied patterns directly into `bash -c` format string. LLM could inject arbitrary shell commands. Made worse because `Glob` is classified `ToolEffect::Pure`, so guard hooks skip it.

Requirements:

R1. Replace Shell-Out with `glob` Crate — Add `glob` crate to `Cargo.toml`. Rewrite `glob_exec` using `glob::glob()` or `glob::glob_with()`. No shell subprocess spawned under any input.

R2. Preserve Existing Behavior — Support `**`, `*`, `?`, `[...]`. Respect `path` parameter as search root. Newline-separated output. Cap at 1000 entries. Empty results return `"No files found"`. Sorting: accept glob crate's alphabetical order (current behavior never sorted by mtime anyway).

R3. Pattern Construction — If pattern starts with `/` or `.`, use as-is. Otherwise prepend base directory: `format!("{base}/{pattern}")`.

R4. Error Handling — `glob::glob()` `PatternError` → `Err(String)`. Individual `GlobError` entries (permission denied) → skip silently, continue.

R5. Brace Expansion Pre-Processing — `glob` crate does not support `{a,b,c}`. Pre-process by detecting and expanding a single top-level brace group before calling `glob::glob()`. Nested braces and multiple groups treated as literals. If `{` found but no matching `}`, pass pattern through unchanged. Any expanded pattern with `PatternError` → fail entire operation (no partial results). 1000-entry limit applies to combined deduplicated results across all expanded patterns.

Success Criteria: no `Command::new("bash")` in `glob_exec`, `**/*.rs` works, `**/*.{rs,toml}` works, invalid pattern returns error (not panic), shell metacharacters treated as literals, `ToolEffect::Pure` classification remains correct, existing tests pass.

Note: use `glob::glob()` (not `glob_with()` with `Default::default()`) — `Default::default()` sets `case_sensitive: false`, which would be a silent behavioral change.

## specs/sse-buffer-optimization.md — Optimize SSE Buffer Reallocation

Status: Active. Target: eliminate unnecessary String allocation in the SSE parser's event extraction loop.

Problem: current code allocates two new Strings per event: `buffer[..pos].to_string()` and `buffer[pos+2..].to_string()`. The second is the main issue — O(N * M) copy work across N events.

Requirements:

R1. Eliminate Remaining-Buffer Reallocation — After extracting an event, advance past it without copying remaining content into a new String. Preferred approach: `buffer.drain(..pos + 2)` — removes consumed bytes in-place with a single `memmove`.

R2. Eliminate Event-Block Allocation — Use slice reference `&buffer[..pos]` and iterate lines directly, avoiding one allocation per event.

R3. Preserve Exact Parsing Behavior — Identical output for all existing SSE test cases. Event boundary detection, `data:` line extraction, JSON parsing, all event type handlers unchanged.

Architecture: use a block scope to end the slice borrow before calling `drain`, since the borrow checker requires the reference is dropped before mutation.

Success Criteria: no `.to_string()` calls on buffer slices in the event extraction loop, all 6 existing SSE parser tests pass unchanged (`parse_sse_text_response`, `parse_sse_tool_use_response`, `parse_sse_error_event_transient`, `parse_sse_error_event_permanent`, `parse_sse_missing_stop_reason`, `parse_sse_usage_from_message_start_and_delta`), clippy clean.

Note: lowest priority of the 5 Phase 2 specs. No dependency on other specs; can be implemented in any order.

## specs/prompt-caching.md — Prompt Caching for System Prompt and Tools

Status: Active. Target: add `cache_control` to system prompt and tool definitions to enable Anthropic prompt caching, reducing input token costs up to 90% on multi-turn conversations.

Background: `Usage` struct already tracks cache token fields; SSE parser already extracts them. The request side never asks for caching.

Requirements:

R1. System Prompt as Cached Content Block — Change `"system"` field in API request body from plain string to an array containing a single text block with `"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}`.

R2. Tool Definitions with Cache Control — Add `"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}` to LAST tool definition only (API caches everything up to and including it). Add in `send_message()` after cloning tools array. Do NOT modify `tools/mod.rs`.

R3. No Change to Response Parsing — `Usage` struct and SSE parser already handle cache token fields. No changes to `parse_sse_stream` or `Usage`.

R4. Verbose Logging — `[verbose] Cache: {cache_read_input_tokens} read, {cache_creation_input_tokens} created, {input_tokens} total input`.

Success Criteria: system prompt sent as array with `cache_control`, last tool has `cache_control`, second API call shows non-zero `cache_read_input_tokens`, first call shows non-zero `cache_creation_input_tokens`, verbose cache stats per call, all existing tests pass, `all_tool_schemas()` returns unmodified schemas.

Implementation uses `clone-and-mutate` approach: `let mut cached_tools = tools.to_vec(); if let Some(last) = cached_tools.last_mut() { last["cache_control"] = json!({"type": "ephemeral"}); }`.

---

## specs/project-instructions-loading.md — Load Project Instructions into System Prompt

Status: Active. Target: read `CLAUDE.md` or `AGENTS.md` at startup and append to system prompt.

Requirements:

R1. Read Project Instructions at Startup — Try `CLAUDE.md` first, then `AGENTS.md`. Use FIRST file found; skip second (often identical via symlink, loading both would duplicate). If neither exists, skip silently. Unreadable file → warning + try next candidate. Follow symlinks.

R2. Prompt Integration — Append after base system prompt with delimiter and section header naming the actual file loaded:

```text
{base prompt}

---

## Project Instructions (from {filename})

{contents}
```

No truncation.

R3. Size Guard — Skip if file exceeds 32KB (32,768 bytes). Log warning: `[warn] {filename} exceeds 32KB, skipping`. Try next candidate after skip.

R4. Verbose Logging — `[verbose] Loaded project instructions from {filename} ({n} bytes)` on success. `[verbose] No CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md found in working directory` if neither found.

Architecture: `enum InstructionsResult { Found { filename, contents }, Skipped { filename, reason }, NotFound }`. Function `load_project_instructions()` does no logging — returns structured data. Caller in `main()` handles all output. If both candidates are skipped, returns last `Skipped` result (deliberate simplicity tradeoff). Dangling symlinks cause `metadata()` to fail with `NotFound` → handled by loop continue.

Success Criteria: CLAUDE.md loaded when present, AGENTS.md loaded when CLAUDE.md absent, CLAUDE.md takes priority when both present, symlinked files work, neither present produces no error, 32KB file skipped with warning, unreadable file produces warning, verbose mode logs loaded filename, section header names actual file, `build_system_prompt()` signature unchanged, all existing tests pass.

---

## specs/run-turn-refactor.md — Extract Shared Tool Dispatch Logic from run_turn

Status: Active. Target: reduce `run_turn` from ~480 lines to ~300 by extracting duplicated pre-hook, dispatch, post-hook, and threshold logic.

Problem: parallel and sequential dispatch paths share ~100 lines of duplicated protocol logic (null-input guard, pre-hook dispatch, block counting, post-hook dispatch, result formatting). The duplication was introduced by tool-parallelism spec.

Requirements:

R1. Extract Pre-Dispatch Protocol — Async function encapsulating: null-input check, pre-hook invocation, block counting, threshold checking. Returns `PreDispatchResult { Allow, Blocked(ContentBlock), ThresholdTripped }`. Null-input → `Blocked` with error ToolResult in BOTH paths (sequential previously silently skipped — intentional unification). Block counting via `&mut` parameters for `consecutive_block_count` and `total_block_count`. `consecutive_block_count` resets to 0 inside function on `Allow`.

R2. Extract Post-Dispatch Protocol — Async function: result formatting/logging, post-hook invocation, signal detection. Signature: `async fn run_post_dispatch(hooks, name, input, content: &str, is_error: bool, iterations, verbose) -> bool`. Takes raw result data (not ContentBlock). Sequential path: borrow `&content` for `run_post_dispatch`, THEN move `content` into ContentBlock. Parallel path: destructure ContentBlock from slot first. Does NOT produce a new ContentBlock.

R3. Preserve Behavior (with one intentional change) — Identical results for all dispatch types, blocked tools, threshold trips, signal breaks. Intentional changes: null-input now produces error ToolResult in both paths; null-input tools now set `blocked_flags` in parallel path (fixes pre-existing bug where post-hooks fired on fabricated results for tools that never executed).

R4. Reduce run_turn Line Count — Target: under 350 lines. Extracted functions live in `main.rs` as private module-level functions.

R5. Maintain clippy::too_many_arguments Suppression — 8-parameter signature unchanged. Keep `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]`.

Success Criteria: all 119 existing tests pass with zero modification, clippy clean, `run_turn` under 350 lines, no duplicated pre/post hook or threshold logic, parallel path still uses `spawn_blocking` + `join_all`, sequential path still dispatches one at a time with streaming callback, hook behavior identical.

Intentional behavioral fixes: null-input unification (error ToolResult in both paths), `blocked_flags` bug fix in parallel path.

Note: `filter_null_input_tool_use` (existing) strips null-input blocks from MaxTokens responses before the dispatch loop; the null-input check inside `run_pre_dispatch` is a safety net for the ToolUse stop_reason case (API anomaly). Both defenses remain.

---

## specs/release-workflow.md — GitHub Release Workflow

Status: Complete. Target: cross-platform release binaries via GitHub Actions, triggered by version tags.

Requirements:

R1. Trigger — Push to version tag matching `v*`. No manual dispatch.

R2. Build Matrix — Two targets: `aarch64-apple-darwin` on `macos-latest` (Apple Silicon native), `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu` on `ubuntu-latest` (native). Each target: `cargo test` then `cargo build --release`.

R3. Artifact Packaging — Binary name: `forgeflare`. Tarball per target: `forgeflare-{tag}-{target}.tar.gz`. Contents: binary only. Compression: gzip.

R4. GitHub Release — Create release from tag after all matrix builds succeed. Attach both tarballs. Title: tag name. Body: auto-generated changelog via `gh release create --generate-notes`. Mark as latest.

R5. CI Gate — Release job depends on existing CI jobs (lint, audit, test, build) passing. No artifacts published if any validation fails.

R6. Consistency with Existing CI — Pin all action SHAs (same convention as `ci.yml`). Use `jdx/mise-action` and `Swatinem/rust-cache`. Least-privilege permissions: `contents: write` only on release creation job; `contents: read` on build jobs.

R7. Cross-Compilation — Both targets are native builds. No cross-compilation toolchain, no `cross` crate, no `cargo-zigbuild`.

File: `.github/workflows/release.yml` (separate from `ci.yml`).

Success Criteria: tag push triggers workflow, both platforms compile and tests pass, both tarballs attached, tarball extracts to working binary, release blocked if CI fails, no artifacts on build failure, SHAs pinned, permissions least-privilege.

Non-Goals: Intel Mac, ARM Linux, .deb/.rpm, Homebrew, auto-versioning, Docker, code signing, `workflow_dispatch`.

Here is a comprehensive summary of every spec, with actual requirement IDs and their technical content.

---

## specs/README.md — Specs Index

This is the master index. It tracks status (Active vs. Complete) for all 15 specs plus two implementation orders:

Phase 1 (Complete, in order): coding-agent, tool-name-compliance, api-endpoint, api-retry, session-capture, maxtoken-continuation, token-aware-trim, tool-parallelism, hooks, release-workflow.

Phase 2 (Active, in order): glob-shell-injection, sse-buffer-optimization, prompt-caching, project-instructions-loading, run-turn-refactor.

---

## specs/coding-agent.md — Unified Rust Coding Agent

Status: Complete. Target: single binary, streaming, <950 production lines.

Requirements:

R1. Core Loop — REPL: read input, call Claude API, dispatch tools, repeat. Streaming SSE from day 1. Conversation history managed as a context array.

R2. HTTP Client — Use `reqwest` directly (no Anthropic crate). POST to `/v1/messages`. Handle SSE. Expose `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.

R3. Tool Registry Pattern — `tools!` macro generates `all_tool_schemas()`. `dispatch_tool()` is hand-written to support per-tool signatures (bash gets a streaming callback, others do not).

R4. Five Tools — Read (file contents, binary/size limits), Glob (path+recursive, file listing), Bash (command+cwd, timeout, streaming via callback), Edit (path+old_str+new_str+replace_all; exact match default; empty old_str on missing file = create with mkdir; empty old_str on existing file = append), Grep (pattern+path+file_type+case_sensitive, shells out to `rg`).

R5. CLI Interface — Single binary. `--verbose`, `--model` (default: claude-opus-4-6), `--max-tokens` (default: 16384, max 128K). Stdin as single prompt if piped. Exit on EOF or "exit".

R6. Error Handling — `thiserror` for `AgentError` in api.rs. Tool errors as `Result<String, String>`. Display errors, continue loop, no panics.

R7. Streaming Architecture — Collect SSE into response buffer. Check `stop_reason` for `tool_use` vs `end_turn`. Dispatch tools or display response accordingly.

R8. Subagent Awareness (Deferred) — No SubagentContext types. `StopReason` enum retained for loop control only.

Success Criteria: binary compiles, tests pass, clippy clean, formatted, can chat/read/list/bash/edit/search, under 950 production lines, streaming visible in real-time.

Notable implementation notes: 720KB byte budget for conversation trim (~180K tokens). Bash deny-list blocks destructive commands (rm -rf /, fork bombs, etc.) with whitespace normalization. 50-iteration tool loop safety limit. 30s connect / 300s request timeouts.

---

## specs/tool-name-compliance.md — Tool Name Compliance

Status: Complete. Target: rename tool names to PascalCase for OAuth proxy compatibility.

Background: OAuth proxy (Claude Max subscription) validates tool names against Claude Code's known set. `"bash"` → 400 error. `"Bash"` → 200 success.

Requirements:

R1. Rename Tool Schemas — In `tools/mod.rs` `tools!` macro: `read_file` → `Read`, `list_files` → `Glob`, `bash` → `Bash`, `edit_file` → `Edit`, `code_search` → `Grep`. Only the `name` field changes.

R2. Update Dispatch Match Arms — Update `dispatch_tool()` match arms from old names to new names.

R3. Update System Prompt — `build_system_prompt()` in `main.rs` must reference new tool names. Model sees names in both schema and system prompt; they must agree.

R4. Update Tests — All tests referencing tool names by string: `schemas_returns_five`, `dispatch_known_tool`/`dispatch_unknown_tool`, SSE parser tests, `system_prompt_contains_environment_info`, `assistant_tool_use` helper in main.rs.

R5. Update AGENTS.md — Tool listing in documentation.

Success Criteria: `all_tool_schemas()` returns names `Read`, `Glob`, `Bash`, `Edit`, `Grep`. OAuth proxy requests with tools succeed. System prompt references new names.

Non-Goals: no behavior changes, no schema changes, no proxy-side mapping.

---

## specs/api-endpoint.md — API Endpoint Configuration

Status: Complete. Target: configurable endpoint defaulting to tailnet OAuth proxy.

Requirements:

R1. Configurable API Base URL — `api_url` field on `AnthropicClient`. Default: `https://anthropic-oauth-proxy.tailfb3ea.ts.net`. Override via `--api-url` CLI arg or `ANTHROPIC_API_URL` env var. Precedence: CLI > env > default. `/v1/messages` path appended at request time, not stored.

R2. Optional API Key — `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` becomes optional. When set: attach `x-api-key` header (passthrough mode). When unset: no auth header (OAuth pool mode). Remove `MissingApiKey` error variant.

R3. CLI Integration — Add `--api-url` to `Cli` struct via clap. Default from `ANTHROPIC_API_URL` env var, falling back to proxy URL.

R4. AnthropicClient Construction — `new()` accepts `api_url: &str`. Store `api_url: String` and `api_key: Option<String>`. No URL validation at construction.

R5. Request Construction — Endpoint: `format!("{}/v1/messages", self.api_url)`. Conditionally attach `x-api-key`. Always send `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01`.

Success Criteria: `cargo run` on tailnet works with zero env vars. Direct API key mode works. `--api-url` in `--help`. `--verbose` prints resolved API URL at startup.

---

## specs/api-retry.md — API Retry for Transient Failures

Status: Complete. Target: retry transient errors (429, 503, 529, network timeouts) with exponential backoff.

Supersedes coding-agent.md's "No automatic retry" principle.

Requirements:

R1. Classify API Errors as Transient or Permanent — By variant:
- `HttpError { status, .. }`: transient = 429, 503, 529, unknown >= 500; permanent = 400, 401, 403, unknown 4xx
- `StreamTransient(String)`: always transient. Covers SSE `error` events with `overloaded_error`, `api_error` types; stream ended without stop_reason
- `StreamParse(String)`: always permanent. Malformed JSON, protocol violations, `invalid_request_error` SSE events
- `Api(reqwest::Error)`: transient if `e.is_timeout()` or `e.is_connect()`; otherwise permanent
- `Json(serde_json::Error)`: always permanent

SSE error classification: inspect `p["error"]["type"]` (nested, NOT top-level `p["type"]`). `overloaded_error`, `api_error`, `rate_limit_error` → `StreamTransient`. `invalid_request_error` → `StreamParse`. Unknown types default to `StreamTransient`.

R2. Retry with Exponential Backoff — Backoff schedule: 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s. Max 4 retries (5 total calls). If `HttpError` has `retry_after: Some(n)`, use n seconds instead (capped at 60s). After max retries: fall through to existing error handling.

R3. Preserve Existing Error Path for Permanent Failures — Permanent errors: immediate `recover_conversation()` + break. No behavioral change for 400/401/403.

R4. Logging — `[retry] Attempt {n}/4: {status} — waiting {delay}s` (1-indexed). `[retry] Using retry-after: {n}s` when header overrides backoff.

Architecture: Add `HttpError { status: u16, retry_after: Option<u64>, body: String }` variant to `AgentError`. Add `StreamTransient(String)` variant. Add `classify_error(e: &AgentError) -> ErrorClass` in api.rs. Retry loop lives in main.rs, wraps entire `send_message()` call (not inside Err arm).

Success Criteria: 429 retried after retry-after seconds, 503/529 with exponential backoff, network timeout retried, 400 triggers immediate break, max 4 retries, attempts logged, `recover_conversation()` still called on final failure.

---

## specs/session-capture.md — Session Transcript Persistence

Status: Complete. Target: persist conversation transcripts to Entire-compatible JSONL.

Requirements:

R1. JSONL Transcript — One JSON object per line to `.entire/metadata/{session-id}/full.jsonl`. User turns: `type: "user"`, `message.role: "user"`. Assistant turns: `type: "assistant"`, `message.role: "assistant"`. Every line includes: `sessionId`, `uuid`, `parentUuid` (null for first line), `timestamp` (ISO 8601 UTC), `cwd`, `version`.

R2. Token Usage — Parse `usage` from `message_start` and `message_delta` SSE events. Fields: `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`. Attach to assistant turn lines as `message.usage`. Return usage from `send_message()` as third element in tuple.

R3. Session Identity — Generate at startup: `{date}-{uuid}` format. UUID v4.

R4. Timestamps — Wall-clock UTC ISO 8601 for every conversation turn. Captures when turn was added, not when displayed.

R5. Supporting Files — `prompt.txt` (initial user prompt), `summary.txt` (not generated, left absent), `context.md` (session metadata header + "Key Actions" list from tool_use blocks: `- **{tool_name}**: {first_arg_value}`).

R6. Write Timing — Append to `full.jsonl` after each complete turn (not at session end). Write `prompt.txt` on first user input (crash-safe). Write `context.md` at session end.

R7. Directory Structure — Create `.entire/metadata/{session-id}/` on first write. Create intermediate dirs. Respect existing `.entire/` directory.

Success Criteria: JSONL written incrementally, token usage parsed and attached, date-uuid session ID, ISO 8601 timestamps, prompt.txt written on first input, context.md has metadata and key actions, existing `.entire/` state not modified, no performance regression, valid JSON per line.

---

## specs/token-aware-trim.md — Token-Aware Context Trimming

Status: Complete. Target: use actual API token counts to gate context trim decisions.

Requirements:

R1. Track Last Known Input Tokens — After each successful `send_message()`, store `usage.input_tokens` as `last_input_tokens: u64`. Initialize to 0 at start of each outer loop iteration.

R2. Token-Based Trim Decision — Before calling `trim_conversation()`:
- `last_input_tokens == 0`: fall through to byte-based trim (no token data yet, or usage absent)
- `last_input_tokens > 0 && last_input_tokens < TRIM_THRESHOLD` (120K): skip trim entirely
- `last_input_tokens >= TRIM_THRESHOLD`: run `trim_conversation()`

R3. Context Window Constant — `MODEL_CONTEXT_TOKENS: u64 = 200_000`. `TRIM_THRESHOLD: u64 = MODEL_CONTEXT_TOKENS * 60 / 100` (120K tokens). 60% threshold leaves 80K headroom for tool results. Do NOT raise to 80% — that leaves only 40K headroom, which large file reads can exceed.

R4. Preserve Byte-Based Safety Net — `trim_conversation()` itself unchanged. Token check is a gate before the byte trim, not a replacement.

Success Criteria: first API call always runs byte trim, subsequent calls skip trim when under 120K tokens, run trim when at or above 120K, `last_input_tokens` resets to 0 on new user input, existing trim tests pass.

---

## specs/maxtoken-continuation.md — MaxTokens Continuation

Status: Complete. Target: continue from MaxTokens truncation instead of breaking the inner loop.

Requirements:

R1. Detect Continuable Truncation — After MaxTokens handling (filter null-input tool_use blocks, inject placeholder if empty): check if remaining content is text-only (no pending tool_use blocks with valid inputs). Text-only = continuable. Tool_use blocks present = fall through to dispatch.

R2. Inject Continuation Prompt — Push User message "Continue from where you left off." AND log via `session.append_user_turn()`. Do NOT increment `tool_iterations`. If response is empty after filtering (only placeholder), do NOT attempt continuation — break immediately. Loop back to API call.

R3. Cap Continuations — Maximum 3 continuation attempts. Track via `continuation_count`. After 3, fall through to existing break.

R4. Logging — `[continue] Response truncated at max_tokens, requesting continuation ({n}/3)`. `[continue] Max continuations reached, breaking`.

Architecture (canonical control flow, exact restructure specified):

```text
if stop_reason == EndTurn { break; }
if stop_reason == MaxTokens {
    // filter null-input + inject placeholder (existing code)
    if is_empty { break; }
    let has_valid_tools = ...;
    if has_valid_tools { /* fall through to dispatch */ }
    else if continuation_count < 3 { continuation_count += 1; /* push user msg, log, continue */ }
    else { /* log cap, break */ }
}
// Tool dispatch runs for ToolUse and MaxTokens-with-valid-tools
```

Success Criteria: text-only MaxTokens triggers continuation, tool_use MaxTokens falls to dispatch, continuation itself hitting MaxTokens increments count toward cap, empty MaxTokens breaks immediately, `continuation_count` resets on new user input (outer loop), continuation logged to JSONL via `session.append_user_turn()`, cap at 3, `continuation_count` does NOT increment `tool_iterations`, does NOT reset on tool_result User messages.

---

## specs/tool-parallelism.md — Parallel Dispatch for Pure Tools

Status: Complete. Target: classify tools by side effects, execute read-only tools concurrently.

Requirements:

R1. Tool Effect Classification — `pub ToolEffect` enum: `Pure` and `Mutating`. Map: `Read → Pure`, `Glob → Pure`, `Grep → Pure`, `Bash → Mutating`, `Edit → Mutating`. `pub fn tool_effect(name: &str) -> ToolEffect` in `tools/mod.rs`. Unknown tool names default to `Mutating`.

R2. Batch Classification — Before dispatching: if ALL tools in batch are `Pure`, dispatch concurrently. If ANY tool is `Mutating`, dispatch entire batch sequentially.

R3. Concurrent Dispatch — Use `futures_util::future::join_all()` (from `futures_util`, NOT `futures` umbrella crate). Parallel path MUST replicate null-input guard. Log each tool name BEFORE spawning. Collect results in original order. Failed individual tool does not cancel siblings.

R4. Preserve Sequential Fallback — `Mutating` path is exactly the existing `for block in ...` loop. Bash streaming callback continues as-is.

Architecture: `dispatch_tool()` is sync; wrap in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || dispatch_tool(...))`. Streaming callback is `&mut dyn FnMut(&str)` (not Send), so pure path uses no-op `&mut |_: &str| {}` inside closures. `join_all` returns `Vec<Result<ContentBlock, JoinError>>`. `tool_iterations += 1` must be present after BOTH parallel and sequential paths. The `tool_results.is_empty()` break guard preserved after both paths.

Success Criteria: 3 concurrent Reads complete faster than sequential, mixed batch dispatches sequentially, results maintain original ordering, individual tool errors don't cancel siblings, unknown tool names classified as Mutating, `JoinError` produces valid ToolResult error, per-tool logging present in parallel path, batch of 1 pure tool works.

---

## specs/hooks.md — Hook Dispatch Layer

Status: Complete. Target: shell-based hook system with PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop events.

Requirements:

R1. Three Hook Events — `PreToolUse` (before execution; guard can allow/block; observe always runs), `PostToolUse` (after execution; can signal convergence), `Stop` (on loop end; 7 reason values: `end_turn`, `iteration_limit`, `api_error`, `continuation_cap`, `block_limit_consecutive`, `block_limit_total`, `convergence_signal`).

R2. Hook Configuration — TOML at `.forgeflare/hooks.toml`. Fields: `event`, `command`, `match_tool` (optional, exact match only), `phase` (`guard`/`observe`, PreToolUse only), `timeout_ms` (default 5000, Stop hooks default 3000). Missing file → no-op zero overhead.

R3. Hook Contract — Executables. JSON on stdin, JSON on stdout. Stderr forwarded. Exit 0 = valid JSON. Non-zero = failure. `tool_iterations` is per-batch counter (same counter as 50-iteration ceiling, increments once per API response cycle). PostToolUse `result` field capped at 5120 bytes with truncation marker (first 2560 + `"\n... (truncated...)...\n"` + last 2560, byte boundaries via `floor_char_boundary`).

R4. PreToolUse Phase Model — Guard hooks: can return `allow` or `block`; run in declaration order; short-circuit on block; fail-closed (timeout/non-zero exit/invalid JSON → Block). Observe hooks: always run even after guard block; receive `blocked`/`blocked_by`/`block_reason` fields; return value ignored; fail-open. Distinct error messages for: intentional block vs. timeout vs. crash vs. invalid JSON.

R5. PostToolUse and Stop are Fail-Open — Failures logged, proceed.

R6. Block Counters with Inner Loop Break — `consecutive_block_count`: increments on every guard block, resets to 0 on every successful dispatch, threshold `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_BLOCKS = 3`. `total_block_count`: increments on every guard block, never resets within inner loop, threshold `MAX_TOTAL_BLOCKS = 10`. When threshold hit: `conversation.pop()` (NOT `recover_conversation()`), break. Aborted batch does NOT increment `tool_iterations`. Block threshold takes unconditional precedence over `signal_break`.

R7. PostToolUse Signal as Control Flow — `PostToolResult::Signal` → set `signal_break` flag. After batch completes, send tool_results, then break WITHOUT `recover_conversation()`. Stop hook fires with `convergence_signal`. Multiple PostToolUse hooks run in order; first Signal controls return value but subsequent hooks still execute and write observations.

R8. Convergence State Protocol — `.forgeflare/convergence.json` with `observations` array (PostToolUse signals) and `final` key (Stop). Deleted at startup (clean slate). Atomic writes via same-directory temp file + `fs::rename` (NOT `/tmp/`). Single read-modify-write per `run_post_tool_use` call after all hooks complete. Write failures logged as warnings, do not affect return value.

R9. Hooks Wrap Dispatch, Not Inside It — `dispatch_tool` remains sync. Hook calls in `main.rs`. Sequential path: guard → observe → dispatch or block → post-hook. Parallel path: pre-allocate `Vec<Option<ContentBlock>>` slots + `blocked_flags: Vec<bool>`; guard/observe per tool sequentially before spawning; `join_all` dispatched futures; post-hook loop skipping blocked slots. `consecutive_block_count` resets at guard-allow time (before spawn), not execution-completion time.

Architecture changes: new `src/hooks.rs` (~250 LOC), `main.rs` delta (~60 LOC), add `toml` to Cargo.toml. `dispatch_tool` signature unchanged.

Success Criteria: 52 explicit criteria covering guard blocking, fail-closed behavior, observe hook behavior, PostToolUse signals, Stop reasons, block counters, convergence state atomicity, parallel path slot ordering, `match_tool` exact matching, and more.

---

## specs/glob-shell-injection.md — Replace Shell Injection in Glob Tool

Status: Active. Target: eliminate shell injection in `glob_exec` by replacing bash shell-out with the `glob` Rust crate.

Problem: `glob_exec` interpolates user-supplied patterns directly into `bash -c` format string. LLM could inject arbitrary shell commands. Made worse because `Glob` is classified `ToolEffect::Pure`, so guard hooks skip it.

Requirements:

R1. Replace Shell-Out with `glob` Crate — Add `glob` crate to `Cargo.toml`. Rewrite `glob_exec` using `glob::glob()` or `glob::glob_with()`. No shell subprocess spawned under any input.

R2. Preserve Existing Behavior — Support `**`, `*`, `?`, `[...]`. Respect `path` parameter as search root. Newline-separated output. Cap at 1000 entries. Empty results return `"No files found"`. Sorting: accept glob crate's alphabetical order (current behavior never sorted by mtime anyway).

R3. Pattern Construction — If pattern starts with `/` or `.`, use as-is. Otherwise prepend base directory: `format!("{base}/{pattern}")`.

R4. Error Handling — `glob::glob()` `PatternError` → `Err(String)`. Individual `GlobError` entries (permission denied) → skip silently, continue.

R5. Brace Expansion Pre-Processing — `glob` crate does not support `{a,b,c}`. Pre-process by detecting and expanding a single top-level brace group before calling `glob::glob()`. Nested braces and multiple groups treated as literals. If `{` found but no matching `}`, pass pattern through unchanged. Any expanded pattern with `PatternError` → fail entire operation (no partial results). 1000-entry limit applies to combined deduplicated results across all expanded patterns.

Success Criteria: no `Command::new("bash")` in `glob_exec`, `**/*.rs` works, `**/*.{rs,toml}` works, invalid pattern returns error (not panic), shell metacharacters treated as literals, `ToolEffect::Pure` classification remains correct, existing tests pass.

Note: use `glob::glob()` (not `glob_with()` with `Default::default()`) — `Default::default()` sets `case_sensitive: false`, which would be a silent behavioral change.

---

## specs/sse-buffer-optimization.md — Optimize SSE Buffer Reallocation

Status: Active. Target: eliminate unnecessary String allocation in the SSE parser's event extraction loop.

Problem: current code allocates two new Strings per event: `buffer[..pos].to_string()` and `buffer[pos+2..].to_string()`. The second is the main issue — O(N * M) copy work across N events.

Requirements:

R1. Eliminate Remaining-Buffer Reallocation — After extracting an event, advance past it without copying remaining content into a new String. Preferred approach: `buffer.drain(..pos + 2)` — removes consumed bytes in-place with a single `memmove`.

R2. Eliminate Event-Block Allocation — Use slice reference `&buffer[..pos]` and iterate lines directly, avoiding one allocation per event.

R3. Preserve Exact Parsing Behavior — Identical output for all existing SSE test cases. Event boundary detection, `data:` line extraction, JSON parsing, all event type handlers unchanged.

Architecture: use a block scope to end the slice borrow before calling `drain`, since the borrow checker requires the reference is dropped before mutation.

Success Criteria: no `.to_string()` calls on buffer slices in the event extraction loop, all 6 existing SSE parser tests pass unchanged (`parse_sse_text_response`, `parse_sse_tool_use_response`, `parse_sse_error_event_transient`, `parse_sse_error_event_permanent`, `parse_sse_missing_stop_reason`, `parse_sse_usage_from_message_start_and_delta`), clippy clean.

Note: lowest priority of the 5 Phase 2 specs. No dependency on other specs; can be implemented in any order.

---

## specs/prompt-caching.md — Prompt Caching for System Prompt and Tools

Status: Active. Target: add `cache_control` to system prompt and tool definitions to enable Anthropic prompt caching, reducing input token costs up to 90% on multi-turn conversations.

Background: `Usage` struct already tracks cache token fields; SSE parser already extracts them. The request side never asks for caching.

Requirements:

R1. System Prompt as Cached Content Block — Change `"system"` field in API request body from plain string to an array containing a single text block with `"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}`.

R2. Tool Definitions with Cache Control — Add `"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}` to LAST tool definition only (API caches everything up to and including it). Add in `send_message()` after cloning tools array. Do NOT modify `tools/mod.rs`.

R3. No Change to Response Parsing — `Usage` struct and SSE parser already handle cache token fields. No changes to `parse_sse_stream` or `Usage`.

R4. Verbose Logging — `[verbose] Cache: {cache_read_input_tokens} read, {cache_creation_input_tokens} created, {input_tokens} total input`.

Success Criteria: system prompt sent as array with `cache_control`, last tool has `cache_control`, second API call shows non-zero `cache_read_input_tokens`, first call shows non-zero `cache_creation_input_tokens`, verbose cache stats per call, all existing tests pass, `all_tool_schemas()` returns unmodified schemas.

Implementation uses `clone-and-mutate` approach: `let mut cached_tools = tools.to_vec(); if let Some(last) = cached_tools.last_mut() { last["cache_control"] = json!({"type": "ephemeral"}); }`.

---

## specs/project-instructions-loading.md — Load Project Instructions into System Prompt

Status: Active. Target: read `CLAUDE.md` or `AGENTS.md` at startup and append to system prompt.

Requirements:

R1. Read Project Instructions at Startup — Try `CLAUDE.md` first, then `AGENTS.md`. Use FIRST file found; skip second (often identical via symlink, loading both would duplicate). If neither exists, skip silently. Unreadable file → warning + try next candidate. Follow symlinks.

R2. Prompt Integration — Append after base system prompt with delimiter and section header naming the actual file loaded:

```text
{base prompt}

---

## Project Instructions (from {filename})

{contents}
```

No truncation.

R3. Size Guard — Skip if file exceeds 32KB (32,768 bytes). Log warning: `[warn] {filename} exceeds 32KB, skipping`. Try next candidate after skip.

R4. Verbose Logging — `[verbose] Loaded project instructions from {filename} ({n} bytes)` on success. `[verbose] No CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md found in working directory` if neither found.

Architecture: `enum InstructionsResult { Found { filename, contents }, Skipped { filename, reason }, NotFound }`. Function `load_project_instructions()` does no logging — returns structured data. Caller in `main()` handles all output. If both candidates are skipped, returns last `Skipped` result (deliberate simplicity tradeoff). Dangling symlinks cause `metadata()` to fail with `NotFound` → handled by loop continue.

Success Criteria: CLAUDE.md loaded when present, AGENTS.md loaded when CLAUDE.md absent, CLAUDE.md takes priority when both present, symlinked files work, neither present produces no error, 32KB file skipped with warning, unreadable file produces warning, verbose mode logs loaded filename, section header names actual file, `build_system_prompt()` signature unchanged, all existing tests pass.

---

## specs/run-turn-refactor.md — Extract Shared Tool Dispatch Logic from run_turn

Status: Active. Target: reduce `run_turn` from ~480 lines to ~300 by extracting duplicated pre-hook, dispatch, post-hook, and threshold logic.

Problem: parallel and sequential dispatch paths share ~100 lines of duplicated protocol logic (null-input guard, pre-hook dispatch, block counting, post-hook dispatch, result formatting). The duplication was introduced by tool-parallelism spec.

Requirements:

R1. Extract Pre-Dispatch Protocol — Async function encapsulating: null-input check, pre-hook invocation, block counting, threshold checking. Returns `PreDispatchResult { Allow, Blocked(ContentBlock), ThresholdTripped }`. Null-input → `Blocked` with error ToolResult in BOTH paths (sequential previously silently skipped — intentional unification). Block counting via `&mut` parameters for `consecutive_block_count` and `total_block_count`. `consecutive_block_count` resets to 0 inside function on `Allow`.

R2. Extract Post-Dispatch Protocol — Async function: result formatting/logging, post-hook invocation, signal detection. Signature: `async fn run_post_dispatch(hooks, name, input, content: &str, is_error: bool, iterations, verbose) -> bool`. Takes raw result data (not ContentBlock). Sequential path: borrow `&content` for `run_post_dispatch`, THEN move `content` into ContentBlock. Parallel path: destructure ContentBlock from slot first. Does NOT produce a new ContentBlock.

R3. Preserve Behavior (with one intentional change) — Identical results for all dispatch types, blocked tools, threshold trips, signal breaks. Intentional changes: null-input now produces error ToolResult in both paths; null-input tools now set `blocked_flags` in parallel path (fixes pre-existing bug where post-hooks fired on fabricated results for tools that never executed).

R4. Reduce run_turn Line Count — Target: under 350 lines. Extracted functions live in `main.rs` as private module-level functions.

R5. Maintain clippy::too_many_arguments Suppression — 8-parameter signature unchanged. Keep `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]`.

Success Criteria: all 119 existing tests pass with zero modification, clippy clean, `run_turn` under 350 lines, no duplicated pre/post hook or threshold logic, parallel path still uses `spawn_blocking` + `join_all`, sequential path still dispatches one at a time with streaming callback, hook behavior identical.

Intentional behavioral fixes: null-input unification (error ToolResult in both paths), `blocked_flags` bug fix in parallel path.

Note: `filter_null_input_tool_use` (existing) strips null-input blocks from MaxTokens responses before the dispatch loop; the null-input check inside `run_pre_dispatch` is a safety net for the ToolUse stop_reason case (API anomaly). Both defenses remain.

---

## specs/release-workflow.md — GitHub Release Workflow

Status: Complete. Target: cross-platform release binaries via GitHub Actions, triggered by version tags.

Requirements:

R1. Trigger — Push to version tag matching `v*`. No manual dispatch.

R2. Build Matrix — Two targets: `aarch64-apple-darwin` on `macos-latest` (Apple Silicon native), `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu` on `ubuntu-latest` (native). Each target: `cargo test` then `cargo build --release`.

R3. Artifact Packaging — Binary name: `forgeflare`. Tarball per target: `forgeflare-{tag}-{target}.tar.gz`. Contents: binary only. Compression: gzip.

R4. GitHub Release — Create release from tag after all matrix builds succeed. Attach both tarballs. Title: tag name. Body: auto-generated changelog via `gh release create --generate-notes`. Mark as latest.

R5. CI Gate — Release job depends on existing CI jobs (lint, audit, test, build) passing. No artifacts published if any validation fails.

R6. Consistency with Existing CI — Pin all action SHAs (same convention as `ci.yml`). Use `jdx/mise-action` and `Swatinem/rust-cache`. Least-privilege permissions: `contents: write` only on release creation job; `contents: read` on build jobs.

R7. Cross-Compilation — Both targets are native builds. No cross-compilation toolchain, no `cross` crate, no `cargo-zigbuild`.

File: `.github/workflows/release.yml` (separate from `ci.yml`).

Success Criteria: tag push triggers workflow, both platforms compile and tests pass, both tarballs attached, tarball extracts to working binary, release blocked if CI fails, no artifacts on build failure, SHAs pinned, permissions least-privilege.

Non-Goals: Intel Mac, ARM Linux, .deb/.rpm, Homebrew, auto-versioning, Docker, code signing, `workflow_dispatch`.
